title: Dynamical cooling driven by self-similar fronts in the 2D nonlinear Schrödinger model
authors: Jason Laurie, Simon Thalabard, Sergey Nazarenko
abstract: We analyze the dynamics towards partial thermalization and subsequent cooling in the defocusing two-dimensional nonlinear Schrödinger model, using direct simulations and insights from the wave-kinetic equations (WKE) and a fourth-order differential approximation model (DAM). We show that the evolving WKE spectrum exhibits two distinct similarity ranges--the quasi-thermal core and the ultraviolet tail--whereas in the DAM, an additional range of infrared self-similarity appears. By stretching the quasi-thermal region, the self-similar fronts drive an effective dynamical cooling process towards the formal but ill-defined equilibrium state at vanishing temperature--analogous to an ultraviolet catastrophe in a system of classical waves.
summary: The paper explores how the 2D nonlinear Schrödinger model undergoes a cooling process toward a zero-temperature equilibrium. It demonstrates that this evolution is driven by self-similar spectral fronts and follows specific algebraic scaling laws. The study also highlights the role of non-local interactions in preventing sharp spectral boundaries.
main_problem: Investigating the temporal evolution of the wave action spectrum towards partial thermalization and the subsequent 'dynamical cooling' process in the 2D nonlinear Schrödinger model.
main_result: The study identifies an algebraic cooling law where temperature scales as T(t) proportional to t^(-1/3) and characterizes the self-similar propagation of spectral fronts, distinguishing between first-kind and third-kind self-similarity.
method: The authors use direct numerical simulations of the NLS equation, analysis of the Wave-Kinetic Equation (WKE), and a fourth-order Differential Approximation Model (DAM) combined with self-similar ansatz and asymptotic analysis.
model_or_system: The 2D defocusing nonlinear Schrödinger (NLS) model, which describes physical systems like nonlinear optics (Kerr media), water-wave theory, and Bose-Einstein condensates.
key_observables: Wave action density (N_omega), energy density (E_omega), wave action flux (Q), energy flux (P), temperature (T), and chemical potential (mu).
important_parameters: Frequency (omega), temperature (T), chemical potential (mu), and the spectral exponent (x) determining collision integral convergence.
main_assumptions: Assumptions include the 'super-local' interaction hypothesis in the DAM, the infinite box limit in the WKE, and the use of a power-law spectrum ansatz for convergence analysis.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows spectral evolution toward Rayleigh-Jeans profiles; Figure 2 tracks dynamics of chemical potentials and temperature; Figure 8 and 9 demonstrate numerical evidence for self-similar wave propagation and convergence of weighted profiles.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem of dynamical cooling and the NLS model, moves to numerical and analytical methods (WKE and DAM), presents results on spectral fronts and scaling laws, provides a detailed convergence analysis of the collision integral, and concludes with a discussion on high-frequency nonlocality.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant for many-body dynamics and non-equilibrium statistical mechanics, as it provides a rigorous description of how wave turbulence relaxes toward equilibrium and the emergence of universal scaling laws in classical wave systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
